figures.caption: Figure 2: Residual reduction after a V-cycle for (a) GNN-based smoother and (b) data-driven GMG adapted from Weymouth (2022).

Figure 3: Mean GNN-based smoother (a) relative solve times and (b) relative setup times of 3 models normalized against the mean relaxed Jacobi solve time for 25 circle, TGV, and donut problems. Test dataset grids are 512× 256, 643 and 128× 642, respectively.

Figure 4: Relative solve time of GNN-based smoother against relaxed Jacobi for 10 models tested in canonical CFD problems. Medium problems have approximately 5-6 more cells than the training dataset, while fine around 20 times more. The cross mark is the mean of the set, and the thick line symbolizes the interquartile range, from the first percentile to the third.
Figure 5: Relative solve time of data-driven smoother against relaxed Jacobi for 10 models tested in canonical CFD problems. Models are trained without channel and convection-diffusion problems.

Figure 6: Performance of the best AMG-GNN model in the AirfRANS dataset versus the relaxed Jacobi baseline in terms of (a) average number of V-cycles per batch and (b) average total time per batch. Test batch size is 100.
Table 5: Hyperparameters for the GNN model architecture.
Table 6: Relative solve times for several GCIN configurations.
Table 7: Hyperparameters for data-driven AMG smoother training
Figure 7: History of train and validation loss for the structured dataset.
Figure 8: History of train and validation loss for the unstructured dataset. The learning rate is displayed below to show the effect of the scheduler.

Figure 9: Mean GNN-based smoother (a) relative solve times and (b) relative setup times of 3 trained models normalized against the relaxed Jacobi solve time for 25 circle, TGV and donut problems. The GCIN hidden dimension is 32. Test dataset grids are 512× 256, 643 and 128× 642, respectively.
Table 8: CFD setup per case. Density is ρ = 1.
Table 9: Boundary conditions per case (axes as in the dataset).
Figure 10: Example of meshes used throughout the study are (a) structured, (b) triangle Delaunay, (c) quaddominant, (d) AirfRANS far view and (e) AirfRANS zoomed view.
Figure 11: Canonical flow fields in structured meshes for (a) Poiseuille, (b) channel, (c) convection-diffusion and (d) flat plate flows.
